quiz_questions.question_text: What type of friction acts on an object that is not moving?

options.text: Static friction keeps it still
Kinetic friction keeps it still
Rolling friction keeps it still
Air resistance keeps it still

quiz_questions.explanation: Static friction acts on objects that are not moving relative to the surface. It resists the start of motion and must be overcome before the object can begin sliding.

quiz_questions.question_text: What type of friction acts on an object that is already sliding?

options.text: Static friction on the object
Kinetic friction on the object
Gravitational friction on it
Magnetic friction on the object

quiz_questions.explanation: Kinetic friction acts on objects that are already in motion (sliding) relative to a surface. It opposes the direction of sliding and is generally less than the maximum static friction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is generally greater: maximum static friction or kinetic friction?

options.text: Kinetic friction is always greater than static
They are always exactly equal to each other
Maximum static friction is generally greater
Neither type of friction is ever greater

quiz_questions.explanation: Maximum static friction is generally greater than kinetic friction. This is why it takes more force to start pushing a heavy box than to keep it sliding once it is already moving.

quiz_questions.question_text: What causes friction between two surfaces?

options.text: The color difference between the two surfaces
The temperature difference between surfaces
Microscopic roughness and interlocking of surfaces
The magnetic attraction between the surfaces

quiz_questions.explanation: Friction is caused by the microscopic roughness of surfaces. Even surfaces that look smooth have tiny bumps and ridges that interlock and resist sliding when pressed together.

quiz_questions.question_text: In which direction does friction act relative to the motion or attempted motion of an object?

options.text: In the same direction as the motion or push
In the opposite direction of the motion or push
Perpendicular to the direction of the motion
Friction does not have a specific direction

quiz_questions.explanation: Friction always opposes motion or the tendency to move. If you push a box to the right, friction pushes to the left. If the box slides left, friction pushes right.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to friction if you increase the weight pressing two surfaces together?

options.text: Friction decreases with more weight
Friction stays the same regardless
Friction increases with more weight
Friction disappears with more weight

quiz_questions.explanation: Increasing the weight (normal force) pressing surfaces together increases friction. The microscopic bumps interlock more tightly, making it harder to slide. This is why heavier objects are harder to push.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which surface combination would likely have the most friction?

options.text: Ice sliding on polished glass surface
Rubber sliding on rough concrete surface
Oil sliding on a smooth metal surface
Teflon sliding on Teflon surface pair

quiz_questions.explanation: Rubber on rough concrete has very high friction because both surfaces are rough and rubber deforms to grip the surface. This is why car tires are made of rubber - they need strong friction for traction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why is it harder to start pushing a heavy box than to keep it sliding?

options.text: The box gets lighter once it starts moving
Gravity increases when the box starts moving
Static friction is greater than kinetic friction
The floor becomes smoother during the motion

quiz_questions.explanation: Maximum static friction is greater than kinetic friction. You need more force to overcome static friction and get the box moving. Once sliding, kinetic friction is lower, so less force is needed to keep it going.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does friction always slow objects down?

options.text: No, friction can also help objects start moving forward
Yes, friction always decreases the speed of objects
Yes, friction is only a force that slows things down
No, friction has no effect on motion whatsoever

quiz_questions.explanation: Friction does not always slow objects down. When you walk, static friction between your shoe and the ground pushes you forward. Without friction, you could not walk or drive at all.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to the friction force when you push a stationary object with gradually increasing force?

options.text: Friction stays zero until the object starts moving
Static friction increases to match the push until it breaks
Friction immediately reaches its maximum possible value
Friction decreases as the applied force gets stronger

quiz_questions.explanation: Static friction adjusts to match the applied force, keeping the object stationary. As you push harder, static friction pushes back equally hard. Only when the push exceeds maximum static friction does the object start moving.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is static friction?

options.text: Friction that acts on an object already sliding across a surface
Friction that prevents a stationary object from starting to move
Friction caused by air pushing against a moving object in wind
Friction that occurs only between liquid surfaces in contact

quiz_questions.explanation: Static friction is the friction that prevents a stationary object from starting to move. It acts between surfaces that are not sliding relative to each other and can vary in strength up to a maximum value.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is kinetic friction?

options.text: Friction that keeps objects from starting to move
Friction that acts on objects as they slide along a surface
Friction that only exists in outer space vacuum
Friction caused by magnetic fields between objects

quiz_questions.explanation: Kinetic friction is the friction acting on an object that is already sliding across a surface. It has a constant value for a given pair of surfaces and always opposes the direction of sliding.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'coefficient of friction' mean?

options.text: A number that describes how rough or smooth surfaces are together
The total weight of the object resting on a surface in newtons
The speed at which the object slides across any surface at all
The direction in which the friction force pushes on the object

quiz_questions.explanation: The coefficient of friction is a dimensionless number that represents how much friction exists between two surfaces. Higher values mean more friction. Rubber on concrete has a high coefficient; ice on ice has a low one.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'normal force' mean in the context of friction?

options.text: The perpendicular force a surface exerts on an object
The parallel force that slides the object along a surface
The gravitational attraction between the Earth and Moon
The force of air resistance acting on any moving object

quiz_questions.explanation: The normal force is the force perpendicular to the surface that supports an object. Friction depends directly on the normal force: the greater the normal force, the greater the friction between surfaces.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'traction' mean?

options.text: The grip between a surface and an object due to friction
The speed at which an object slides across a surface
The weight of an object pressing down on a surface
The air resistance acting on a moving vehicle

quiz_questions.explanation: Traction is the grip that friction provides between two surfaces. Good traction means strong friction for control, like rubber tires on dry roads. Poor traction means low friction, like tires on icy roads.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'sliding friction' mean?

options.text: Another name for kinetic friction between surfaces
Friction that keeps objects from starting movement
Friction between wheels and the ground surface
Friction caused by air flowing over the surface

quiz_questions.explanation: Sliding friction is another name for kinetic friction. It occurs when one surface slides over another. It is constant for a given pair of surfaces and always opposes the sliding direction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is meant by 'rolling friction'?

options.text: Friction caused by one object rolling over a surface
Friction between two flat surfaces sliding together
Friction caused by air moving past an object
Friction that prevents any object from moving

quiz_questions.explanation: Rolling friction occurs when a round object rolls over a surface, like a wheel on a road. It is generally much less than sliding (kinetic) friction, which is why wheels make it easier to move heavy loads.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'lubricant' mean in relation to friction?

options.text: A substance that increases friction between surfaces
A substance that reduces friction between surfaces
A substance that measures friction between surfaces
A substance that eliminates gravity on the object

quiz_questions.explanation: A lubricant is a substance (like oil or grease) placed between surfaces to reduce friction. It creates a thin layer that prevents the surfaces from directly touching, reducing the resistance to sliding.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'surface texture' mean in the study of friction?

options.text: The roughness or smoothness of a surface
The color and brightness of a surface
The temperature of the surface material
The weight of the surface material itself

quiz_questions.explanation: Surface texture describes how rough or smooth a surface is. Rougher surfaces have more microscopic bumps that interlock, creating more friction. Smoother surfaces have fewer bumps and less friction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'oppose' mean in the phrase 'friction opposes motion'?

options.text: It acts in the same direction as the motion
It acts against or resists the direction of motion
It has no relationship to the direction of motion
It increases the speed of the motion further

quiz_questions.explanation: To oppose means to act against or resist. Friction opposes motion by pushing in the direction opposite to the object's movement or the direction it would tend to move, slowing it down or preventing motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: You try to push a heavy couch but it does not move. What force is keeping it stationary?

options.text: Kinetic friction is keeping it still
Static friction is keeping it still
Air resistance is keeping it still
Normal force is keeping it still

quiz_questions.explanation: Since the couch is not sliding, static friction is acting. It matches your push force to keep the couch stationary. Only when you push hard enough to exceed maximum static friction will the couch start moving.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hockey puck slides across ice and gradually slows down. What type of friction is acting on it?

options.text: Static friction from the ice surface
There is no friction on ice at all
Kinetic friction from the ice surface
Gravitational friction from the Earth

quiz_questions.explanation: The puck is sliding, so kinetic friction acts on it. Even though ice is very smooth with low friction, there is still some kinetic friction that gradually slows the puck down over time.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do car tires have treads (grooves) on them?

options.text: To make the car look more attractive and stylish
To reduce friction so the car goes faster always
To increase friction and channel away water for grip
To make the tires weigh less overall for the car

quiz_questions.explanation: Tire treads increase friction by providing edges that grip the road. They also channel water away on wet roads, preventing the tire from hydroplaning. This gives the car better traction and control.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student places a book on a tilted board and slowly increases the angle. At what point does the book start sliding?

options.text: When kinetic friction exceeds the normal force on it
When the gravity component along the board exceeds maximum static friction
When the angle reaches exactly 90 degrees vertical
When air resistance pushes the book down the board

quiz_questions.explanation: As the angle increases, the component of gravity pulling the book down the board increases. The book starts sliding when this force exceeds the maximum static friction. Then kinetic friction takes over.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do people put sand on icy sidewalks in winter?

options.text: To increase friction and prevent slipping
To decrease friction for faster walking
To make the ice melt faster in the sun
To make the sidewalk look more clean

quiz_questions.explanation: Sand increases friction on icy surfaces. Ice has very low friction (making it slippery), but sand creates a rougher surface between shoes and ice, providing better traction and preventing people from slipping.

quiz_questions.question_text: A mechanic applies oil to engine parts. How does this affect friction between the parts?

options.text: It increases friction to protect engine parts
It reduces friction so parts slide more easily
It has no effect on friction between the parts
It eliminates all contact between the parts

quiz_questions.explanation: Oil acts as a lubricant, creating a thin layer between metal surfaces that reduces friction. This prevents excessive wear, reduces heat, and allows engine parts to move smoothly against each other.

quiz_questions.question_text: A heavy box requires 50 N to start sliding but only 30 N to keep it sliding. What explains this difference?

options.text: The box becomes lighter once it starts moving
The floor gets smoother after the box passes over it
Gravity decreases once the box begins to move
Static friction (50 N) is greater than kinetic friction (30 N)

quiz_questions.explanation: The 50 N needed to start sliding is the maximum static friction, while the 30 N to keep sliding is kinetic friction. Static friction is greater because stationary surfaces interlock more than sliding ones.

quiz_questions.question_text: A person walks forward. What direction does friction push on their shoes?

options.text: Backward, opposite to walking direction
Straight downward into the ground below
Forward, in the direction of walking
Straight upward away from the ground

quiz_questions.explanation: When walking, your foot pushes backward on the ground. By Newton's third law, friction pushes your foot forward. Static friction between your shoe and ground propels you forward. Without friction, you would slip in place.

quiz_questions.question_text: Brake pads press against a spinning wheel. What type of friction slows the wheel down?

options.text: Static friction between pad and wheel surface
Kinetic friction between pad and wheel surface
Air resistance between the pad and wheel area
Gravitational friction between pad and the wheel

quiz_questions.explanation: When brake pads press against a spinning wheel, the surfaces slide against each other. This is kinetic (sliding) friction, which converts the wheel's kinetic energy into heat, slowing the vehicle down.

quiz_questions.question_text: A parked car sits on a steep hill without rolling. If the hill gets steeper, what eventually happens?

options.text: The car floats upward off the hill surface
Static friction continues to hold the car forever
The car begins to slide when gravity exceeds maximum static friction
The car gets heavier and presses harder into hill

quiz_questions.explanation: As the hill gets steeper, the gravity component pulling the car downhill increases while the normal force decreases. Eventually, gravity exceeds maximum static friction, and the car begins to slide downhill.
